Apple.tagline: The world's most beloved temperate fruit — crunchy, sweet, and endlessly versatile.
Apple.about: Apples originated in the mountains of Central Asia and have been cultivated for thousands of years. Today over 7,500 varieties exist worldwide, ranging from sweet to tart. They are one of the most widely grown fruits globally, prized for fresh eating, juice, and cooking.
micronutrients: Quercetin — powerful flavonoid antioxidant concentrated in the skin.
Pectin — soluble fiber acting as prebiotic for gut bacteria.
Chlorogenic acid — may help lower blood sugar spikes after meals.
benefits: Heart health — flavonoids and fiber reduce LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.
Gut health — pectin feeds beneficial gut bacteria and improves digestion.
Blood sugar control — polyphenols slow carbohydrate digestion.
Weight management — high water and fiber content increases satiety.
cautions: Apple seeds contain amygdalin which releases cyanide — never eat the seeds.
High natural sugar — people with diabetes should eat in moderation.
Apple.india_note: Primarily grown in Himachal Pradesh, J&K, and Uttarakhand. Available year-round via cold storage.
Apple.global_note: Northern hemisphere harvest peaks in autumn; Southern hemisphere ensures near year-round supply.
tips: Pick firm apples with smooth, unbroken skin and no soft spots.
A ripe apple feels heavy for its size.
Store in the refrigerator — apples keep up to 6 weeks when chilled.
Keep away from other produce; apples release ethylene gas that speeds ripening.
culinary_tips: Rub cut apple with lemon juice to prevent browning.
Pairs beautifully with sharp cheddar, walnuts, and honey in salads.
Core and bake with cinnamon, oats, and brown sugar for a simple dessert.
avoid_with: Milk (can cause digestive discomfort for some people)
growing_tips: Requires cold dormancy — not suitable for hot tropical plains.
Best grown in HP, J&K, and Uttarakhand above 1,500m altitude.
Plant two varieties for cross-pollination and better yield.
Prune annually in winter to maintain shape and air circulation.
fun_facts: Apples are 25% air — that's why they float in water.
There are over 7,500 known varieties of apples worldwide.
It takes about 36 apples to produce one gallon of apple cider.

Mango.tagline: The king of fruits — India's most beloved tropical treasure.
Mango.about: Mango has been cultivated in South Asia for over 4,000 years and is the national fruit of India, Pakistan, and the Philippines. India is the world's largest producer, growing over 1,000 varieties. The fruit is revered in Hindu traditions and celebrated across South and Southeast Asia.
micronutrients: Vitamin C — 36.4mg per 100g (61% DV). Boosts immunity and collagen production.
Vitamin A — 54µg. Supports eye health and immune function.
Folate — important for cell division and pregnancy health.
benefits: Eye health — high in lutein and zeaxanthin which protect against macular degeneration.
Immunity — excellent source of Vitamins C and A.
Digestion — contains amylase enzymes that help break down carbohydrates.
Skin health — Vitamin C supports collagen synthesis.
cautions: Very high in natural sugar — diabetics should consume in limited amounts.
Mango sap and skin can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals.
Mango.india_note: Available across India April–July. Alphonso peaks in April–May (Maharashtra), Langra in June–July (UP & Bihar).
Mango.global_note: India produces ~40% of world mangoes. Year-round supply maintained by Southern hemisphere producers in the off-season.
tips: Smell the stem end — ripe mangoes have a strong sweet fragrance.
Gently squeeze — it should give slightly like a ripe peach.
Avoid mangoes with black spots or a fermented smell.
Ripen at room temperature; refrigerate once ripe for up to 5 days.
culinary_tips: Dice into salsa with red onion, jalapeño, lime, and cilantro.
Blend with milk and cardamom for a classic Indian mango lassi.
Slice and add to rice dishes or Thai salads for natural sweetness.
growing_tips: Thrives in tropical and subtropical climates — ideal for most of India.
Needs a dry flowering season (Nov–Jan) followed by good monsoon rain.
Can be grown in large containers using dwarf grafted varieties.
Grafted trees fruit in 3–4 years vs 6–8 years from seed.
fun_facts: India grows over 1,000 varieties of mango.
The mango is the national fruit of India, Pakistan, and the Philippines.
A mango tree can live and bear fruit for over 300 years.

Banana.tagline: Nature's perfect energy bar — portable, sweet, and packed with potassium.
Banana.about: Bananas are one of the world's oldest cultivated fruits, with evidence of cultivation dating back 8,000 years. They are the world's most popular fruit by volume. India is the second-largest producer after China, with hundreds of regional varieties grown across the country.
micronutrients: Potassium — 358mg (10% DV). Critical for heart rhythm and muscle function.
Vitamin B6 — supports brain health and mood regulation.
Magnesium — 27mg. Supports muscle relaxation and sleep quality.
benefits: Energy boost — natural sugars provide quick, sustained energy — ideal pre-workout.
Heart health — high potassium helps maintain healthy blood pressure.
Mood support — tryptophan converts to serotonin, improving mood.
Exercise recovery — replenishes glycogen and electrolytes after workouts.
cautions: Unripe green bananas can cause constipation due to resistant starch.
High in carbohydrates — people managing blood sugar should monitor portions.
Banana.india_note: Available year-round across India. Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, and Andhra Pradesh are the largest producers.
Banana.global_note: Grown year-round in tropical regions worldwide. Exported primarily from Ecuador, Costa Rica, and the Philippines.
tips: Yellow with a few brown spots = peak ripeness and sweetness.
Green bananas are starchy and better cooked (plantain-style).
Store at room temperature — never refrigerate unripe bananas.
Freeze overripe peeled bananas for smoothies and baking.
culinary_tips: Mash overripe bananas into pancake or muffin batter as a natural sweetener.
Slice and freeze for a 2-ingredient nice cream base.
Shallow fry Nendran slices in coconut oil for Kerala-style banana chips.
growing_tips: Grows easily across most of India in warm, humid conditions.
Plant suckers (pups) from the base of an existing plant.
Fruits in 9–12 months; the mother plant dies after fruiting but pups continue.
Keep soil moist but never waterlogged — good drainage is critical.
fun_facts: Bananas are technically berries, while strawberries are not.
A cluster of bananas is called a 'hand'; a single banana is a 'finger'.
Bananas are mildly radioactive due to their natural potassium content.

Apricot.tagline: Sun-kissed stone fruit with velvety skin and tangy-sweet flesh.
Apricot.about: Apricots originated in China over 3,000 years ago and spread via the Silk Road. They're now grown in temperate regions worldwide. Fresh apricots have a delicate floral flavor; dried ones concentrate their sweetness.
micronutrients: Beta-carotene — converts to vitamin A for eye health and immunity.
Vitamin E — protects skin and cells from oxidative damage.
benefits: Eye health — beta-carotene protects against age-related macular degeneration.
Skin health — vitamins A and E promote collagen and fight UV damage.
cautions: Eat pits/spit seeds — contain cyanide compounds like apple seeds.
Dried apricots often contain sulfites — avoid if sensitive.
Apricot.india_note: Grown in Ladakh, Himachal Pradesh, and Uttarakhand; mostly dried form available elsewhere.
Apricot.global_note: Northern hemisphere summer fruit; Chile/Australia supply winter months.
tips: Choose plump apricots with taut skin; avoid wrinkled ones.
They should yield slightly to gentle pressure.
Ripen at room temperature; refrigerate ripe ones up to 5 days.
Store away from strong odors — they absorb flavors.
culinary_tips: Halve and grill with honey for dessert.
Puree with yogurt for Middle Eastern apricot lassi.
Excellent in tagines with lamb and almonds.
growing_tips: Needs 700 chill hours — unsuitable for tropical India.
Plant in full sun with good air circulation.
Thin fruit to prevent branch breakage.
fun_facts: Apricots were once more valuable than gold on the Silk Road.
Turkish apricots are considered the world's best for drying.

Avocado.tagline: Creamy green gold packed with healthy fats and 20+ nutrients.
Avocado.about: Avocados were cultivated by Mesoamericans 10,000 years ago — their name comes from Nahuatl 'ahuacatl' (testicle). Today Mexico produces 80% of global supply. The creamy flesh has more potassium than bananas.
micronutrients: Monounsaturated fats — same heart-healthy fats as olive oil.
Folate — essential for DNA synthesis and pregnancy.
benefits: Heart health — lowers LDL cholesterol and triglycerides.
Eye health — lutein filters harmful blue light.
Skin health — healthy fats improve moisture retention.
cautions: High calorie density — portion control for weight management.
Latex-fruit syndrome — allergy risk for latex-sensitive people.
Avocado.india_note: Grown in Tamil Nadu (Coonoor), Kerala, and Northeast; increasing local supply.
Avocado.global_note: Year-round via staggered harvests; Mexico supplies 60% of world market.
tips: Perfect ripeness when neck yields to gentle pressure.
Buy firm, store at room temp to ripen (3-7 days).
Refrigerate ripe ones up to 1 week.
Speed ripening by bagging with banana or apple.
culinary_tips: Add lime juice immediately after cutting to prevent browning.
Perfect base for sushi rolls and poke bowls.
Blend with cocoa and dates for healthy chocolate mousse.
growing_tips: Protect young trees from frost and strong winds.
Needs well-drained soil — root rot kills more trees than drought.
Fertilize with nitrogen-rich formula during growth.
Graft for commercial varieties — seedling fruit poor.
fun_facts: Avocado flowers have 'general flowering' — both sexes mature together.
One tree produces ~200 avocados/year at peak.
Persian kings chilled avocados in snow 2,000 years ago.

Blueberry.tagline: Tiny indigo superfood bursting with antioxidants and brain benefits.
Blueberry.about: Native to North America, wild blueberries sustained indigenous peoples for millennia. Commercial cultivation began in 1916. They're called 'brain berries' for their cognitive benefits.
micronutrients: Anthocyanins — purple pigments fighting inflammation and aging.
Pterostilbene — resveratrol cousin, more bioavailable for brain.
benefits: Brain health — improves memory, delays cognitive decline.
Heart health — improves arterial function and lowers blood pressure.
Anti-aging — highest antioxidant capacity of common fruits.
Blood sugar control — low glycemic despite natural sugars.
cautions: Organic preferred — conventional have high pesticide residues.
May cause 'blue tongue' and stool discoloration.
Blueberry.india_note: Limited cultivation in Mahabaleshwar and Northeast; mostly imported from Chile/US.
Blueberry.global_note: US produces 70%; Chile/Peru supply southern hemisphere season.
tips: Prime when plump, firm, uniform deep blue-purple.
Refrigerate in ventilated container up to 10 days.
Freeze whole — no washing until use.
culinary_tips: Don't wash before storing — moisture causes mold.
Roast briefly for deeper flavor in sauces.
Layer with lemon curd for stunning parfait.
growing_tips: Requires acidic soil (pH 4.5-5.5) — use peat moss.
Net against birds — they love blueberries too.
Prune oldest canes annually after harvest.
fun_facts: Native Americans called them 'star berries' for five-point calyx.
One cup provides daily antioxidant needs.

Cherry.tagline: Heart-shaped jewels of summer — sweet or tart perfection.
Cherry.about: Cherries trace to the Black Sea region, cultivated since 300 BCE. Sweet cherries dominate fresh market; sour for pies and preserves. They're among the first stone fruits of summer.
benefits: Joint health — reduces gout attacks and arthritis pain.
Heart health — lowers blood pressure and cholesterol.
Cherry.india_note: Limited to Kashmir and Himachal Pradesh; expensive imported varieties common.
Cherry.global_note: Turkey produces 25% world crop; US follows; Chile for winter.
tips: Firm, plump, shiny skin; avoid leaking juice.
Don't wash until eating — moisture shortens life.
culinary_tips: Pit before freezing — expansion cracks skin.
Reduce cherries with balsamic for steak glaze.
Classic clafoutis — batter poured over unpitted cherries.
growing_tips: Bird netting essential from bloom to harvest.
Prune to open center for light penetration.
fun_facts: George Washington didn't chop cherry tree — legend.
Cherries have natural 'rockets' — eject pits up to 30 feet.
National Cherry Festival drops 15,000+ pounds annually.

Coconut.tagline: Tropical tree of life — water, milk, oil, and more from one fruit.
Coconut.about: Called 'tree of life' in Sanskrit, coconuts sustained Pacific islanders. Every part gets used — husk for rope, shell for bowls. India produces 20% of world supply.
benefits: Skin health — moisturizes and has antifungal properties.
cautions: High saturated fat — moderation for cholesterol concerns.
Coconut.india_note: Kerala, TN, Karnataka, AP, Odisha, WB — year-round with peak tender coconut summer.
tips: Heavy for size = more water inside.
Store at room temp 2-3 weeks; refrigerate cut.
culinary_tips: Fresh water best within hours of opening.
Bake flesh into macaroons or bounty bars.
Coconut milk + curry leaves = South Indian tempering.
growing_tips: Dwarf varieties fruit in 4 years vs 8.
fun_facts: Coconut kills more people than sharks (falling).

Dragon Fruit.tagline: Exotic cactus fruit with vibrant pink skin and mild kiwi-like sweetness.
Dragon Fruit.about: Native to Central America, dragon fruit spread via Vietnamese refugees post-war. The cactus blooms only at night, pollinated by bats/moths. Vietnam now produces 50% of global supply.
micronutrients: Betalains — rare red pigments with anti-inflammatory power.
benefits: Gut health — prebiotic fiber promotes healthy microbiome.
Blood sugar control — low glycemic despite sweetness.
Dragon Fruit.india_note: Thriving in Maharashtra, Gujarat, TN, Karnataka; called 'pitaya' locally.
tips: Firm but slightly soft; avoid hard or shriveled.
Refrigerate up to 1 week; ripen at room temp.
Cut shows white flesh with black seeds.
culinary_tips: Scoop flesh, freeze in ice cube trays for drinks.
Toss with lime, mint, chili for Mexican salad.
Natural food dye from red varieties.
growing_tips: Excellent for Madurai climate — drought tolerant.
Plant against south wall for winter warmth.
fun_facts: Bats are primary pollinators in wild.

Durian.tagline: King of fruits — infamously stinky but irresistibly creamy.
Durian.about: Native to Southeast Asia, durian commands premium prices in China. The spiky husk protects creamy pods smelling like sewage/garlic/onion. 'Love it or hate it' defines Asian fruit culture.
benefits: Energy boost — high carbs, low sugar crash.
Durian.india_note: Limited cultivation in Kerala, Northeast; expensive import.
Durian.global_note: Malaysia/Thailand peak June-July; off-season frozen pulp.
tips: Eat fresh same day for best flavor.
culinary_tips: Ice cream version lacks the stink.
fun_facts: Li Shizhen (1578): 'first breath delicious, second heavenly, third kills.'

Fig.tagline: Ancient honey-sweet fruit with crunchy seeds and soft skin.
Fig.about: Figs sustained Mediterranean civilizations for 5,000+ years. Unlike most fruits, the 'fruit' is actually a syconium (hollow stem). California produces 98% of US crop.
benefits: Constipation relief — natural laxative from fiber + sugars.
Fig.india_note: Common in Gujarat, Rajasthan, UP; dried anjeer everywhere.
Fig.global_note: Two crops/year temperate; California/Turkey major producers.
tips: Ripe figs droop heavily, split at base.
Soft but not mushy; neck wrinkles OK.
culinary_tips: Stuff with goat cheese, prosciutto, balsamic.
Fig newtons: blend dried figs + oat crust.
growing_tips: Madurai perfect — drought tolerant once established.
Plant against south wall for heat.

Grape.tagline: Ancient berry cluster — fresh, raisin, wine, or juice.
Grape.about: Domesticated 8,000 years ago in Georgia region. 10,000+ varieties exist. California/India major producers; table grapes distinct from wine grapes.
Grape.india_note: Nashik, Maharashtra #1; 1.5M tons/year; Thompson Seedless dominant.
Grape.global_note: China largest producer; Chile/Peru for winter supply.
tips: Even color, firm but not hard.

Grapefruit.tagline: Bitter-sweet citrus giant born from pomelo-orange hybrid.
Grapefruit.about: Spontaneous pomelo x orange hybrid discovered 1750 Barbados. Ruby Red patented 1929. Bitterness from naringin; pink/red from lycopene.
Grapefruit.india_note: Limited to Nilgiris, Coorg; mostly imported.
Grapefruit.global_note: Florida/US, Israel, South Africa winter crop.
tips: Heavy = juicy; smooth skin over rough.

Guava.tagline: Tropical vitamin C bomb — 5x oranges, crunchy seeds.
Guava.about: Pre-Columbian staple spread by Portuguese worldwide. India produces 20M tons/year. Seeds edible; skin packs most nutrition.
cautions: Eat ripe — unripe causes stomach upset.
Guava.india_note: UP, MH, Bihar, TN — Allahabad Safeda best variety.
culinary_tips: Chaat: roasted cumin, chili, chaat masala.

Jackfruit.tagline: World's largest fruit — meaty vegan 'chicken' substitute.
Jackfruit.about: Native Kerala/Western Ghats; unripe pods pulled pork texture. Ripe sweeter than mango. World's largest tree fruit — single fruit 50kg+.
Jackfruit.india_note: Kerala, TN, Karnataka, Maharashtra; raw in TN curries.
culinary_tips: Raw jack curry Tamil Nadu style.

Kiwi.tagline: Fuzzy vitamin C powerhouse from New Zealand.
Kiwi.about: Chinese gooseberry smuggled to NZ 1904, commercialized 1950s. Now world’s top producer after Italy. Brown fuzzy skin hides jade flesh.
Kiwi.india_note: Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, NE states; increasing production.
tips: Yield to thumb pressure at stem.

Lemon.tagline: Ultimate flavor enhancer and vitamin C powerhouse.
Lemon.about: Assam region origin; Arabs spread to Europe 10th century. India produces 3M tons/year. Essential in every cuisine worldwide.
Lemon.india_note: Andhra Pradesh, MH, Gujarat, Punjab; year-round varieties.
culinary_tips: Nimbu pani: salt + kala namak + cumin.
fun_facts: Vitamin C discovered isolating from lemons.

Lychee.tagline: Delicate floral jewels from China — perfume in fruit form.
Lychee.about: Cultivated 2,000 years China; Mughal emperors prized. India #1 producer 15M tons. Short 4-week season creates frenzy.
fun_facts: Empress Yang Kwei-fei bathed in lychee water.

Melon.about: African/Persian origin; India Kharbuja famous. High water (90%) perfect heatwave fruit. 100+ varieties worldwide.

Orange.tagline: Classic vitamin C citrus — juicy segments perfection.
Orange.about: Chinese origin spread via Silk Road. India Nagpur/Amravati famous. Brazil #1 producer; perfect breakfast fruit.
Orange.india_note: Nagpur, Amravati, Punjab, TN hill oranges.

Papaya.tagline: Tropical enzyme-rich fruit — orange flesh digestion aid.
Papaya.about: Mayan 'tree melon'; India #1 producer 6M tons. Male/female/hermaphrodite trees. Latex contains meat tenderizer papain.
Papaya.india_note: TN, Karnataka, AP, Kerala; Coorg papaya famous.

Peach.tagline: Velvety stone fruit dripping with summer sweetness.
Peach.about: Chinese symbol immortality; Silk Road to Persia (name origin). Freestone/clingstone types. India's Kala Amritsari famous.
Peach.india_note: Himachal Pradesh, J&K, Uttarakhand; Punjab Kala Amritsari.

Pear.tagline: Butter-soft fruit ripening off-tree to perfection.
Pear.about: 5,000-year history; 3,000+ varieties. Pears ripen ethylene from inside. India Shimla Queen local favorite.
Pear.india_note: HP, J&K, Uttarakhand; cold storage year-round.

Pineapple.about: Guarani name 'nana' = excellent fruit. Columbus 1493 Caribbean. India 1.7M tons; TN Vazhakulam #1.

Plum.tagline: Juicy stone fruit spectrum — sweet to culinary tart.
Plum.about: 4,000-year history; 2,000+ varieties. Japanese plums (Prunus salicina) dominate fresh market. India Sant Rohi local.
Plum.india_note: HP, J&K, Uttarakhand; Sant Rohi Punjab favorite.

Pomegranate.tagline: Ruby antioxidant jewels — heart health superfruit.
Pomegranate.about: Persian name 'granatum' = seeded. India Bhagwa variety premium. 600 tons arils daily US Thanksgiving.

Raspberry.tagline: Tiny jewels bursting with tart-sweet flavor and antioxidants.
Raspberry.about: Raspberries have been gathered wild for thousands of years across Europe and Asia. They thrive in temperate climates and are now cultivated worldwide. India grows them in cooler hill stations like Himachal Pradesh and Kashmir.
micronutrients: Vitamin C — 26mg (29% DV). Boosts immunity and skin health.
benefits: Antioxidant power — ellagic acid fights inflammation and cancer risk.
Heart health — fiber lowers cholesterol; anthocyanins improve blood flow.
Weight management — low calories, high fiber keep you full.
cautions: Seeds may irritate diverticulitis; eat seedless jam if sensitive.
High fiber can cause bloating if overeaten.
Raspberry.india_note: Limited to hill regions like Kashmir, Himachal; imported otherwise.
Raspberry.global_note: Peak summer harvest in Northern Hemisphere; Chile supplies off-season.
tips: Pick plump, firm berries that detach easily from core.
Refrigerate in single layer; use within 2-3 days.
Don't wash until ready to eat.
culinary_tips: Blend into smoothies or top yogurt for breakfast.
Make coulis for desserts or sauces.
growing_tips: Needs chill hours; plant in well-drained, acidic soil.
Prune canes annually for better yields.
fun_facts: Raspberries are aggregate fruits with drupelets.
White raspberries exist and are sweeter.

Starfruit.tagline: Star-shaped slices that add crisp, juicy tang to any dish.
Starfruit.about: Native to Southeast Asia, starfruit spread via trade to India and beyond. Its waxy skin and star cross-section make it visually striking. Grown widely in tropical India, especially Maharashtra and Kerala.
cautions: High oxalates — avoid if prone to kidney stones.
Unripe ones are very sour; eat only ripe yellow/orange.
Starfruit.india_note: Common in southern and western India; year-round in tropics.
Starfruit.global_note: Tropical year-round; Taiwan and Florida major exporters.
tips: Choose yellow with slight brown edges for sweetness.
Store in fridge up to a week; slice for star shapes.
culinary_tips: Slice into salads or cocktails for wow factor.
growing_tips: Thrives in humid tropics; protect from strong winds.
Prune for shape; fertilize during fruiting.
fun_facts: Contains low levels of caramboxin, toxic in large amounts to kidney patients.

Strawberry.about: Modern strawberries are hybrids from the Americas and Europe, cultivated since the 1700s. India produces them mainly in subtropical hills like Maharashtra and Kashmir. They're the only fruit with seeds on the outside.
benefits: Blood sugar friendly — low GI despite sweetness.
cautions: Wash well; pesticides common on surface.
Allergy rare but possible (oral allergy syndrome).
Strawberry.india_note: Winter crop in northern hills; imports fill gaps.
Strawberry.global_note: California and Spain lead production; year-round via greenhouses.
tips: Shiny, firm, deep red = ripe; fragrant.
culinary_tips: Macerate with sugar for instant dessert topping.
Roast for intensified flavor in salads.
growing_tips: Mulch plants; rotate to prevent disease.
Needs chill hours for best fruit.
fun_facts: Not a true berry; an aggregate accessory fruit.
One plant can produce 1 quart per season.

Watermelon.tagline: Ultimate hydration hero — 92% water with sweet crunch.
Watermelon.about: Domesticated in Africa 4,000 years ago, watermelons reached India via ancient trade. India is the world's second-largest producer after China. Varieties range from tiny to 100+ lbs.
cautions: Seeds pose choking hazard for kids.
Watermelon.india_note: Summer staple across India; Maharashtra leads production.
tips: Thump for deep sound; yellow spot = ripeness.
Whole in fridge up to 2 weeks; cut lasts 3-5 days.
growing_tips: Vines need space; hill soil for drainage.
Pollinate by hand if bees scarce.
fun_facts: Contains citrulline, a natural Viagra-like compound.

Passion Fruit.tagline: Tropical tang explosion with crunchy seeds.
Passion Fruit.about: Native to Brazil, passion fruit conquered tropics worldwide. India grows purple and yellow varieties in Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu. The flower inspired its 'passion' name from missionaries.
benefits: Digestion aid — fiber and pectins soothe gut.
Sleep support — magnesium and alkaloids calm nerves.
Energy — B vitamins and iron combat fatigue.
cautions: Seeds tough; spit out or strain pulp.
Too much may lower blood pressure.
Passion Fruit.india_note: South India specialty; purple variety most common.
tips: Wrinkled skin = peak ripeness and flavor.
culinary_tips: Scoop pulp into yogurt or cheesecake.

Mulberry.tagline: Silk road superfruit — sweet, juicy, and staining.
Mulberry.about: Cultivated in China for 4,000+ years for silkworms and fruit. Mulberries grow wild and cultivated across India, especially Uttar Pradesh. Stains everything purple — nature's dye.
cautions: Stains clothes/skin badly — eat over sink.
Mulberry.india_note: Common in northern plains; wild in forests.
fun_facts: Berries used as natural food dye.

Mandarin.tagline: Easy-peel citrus sunshine in every segment.
Mandarin.about: Originating in ancient China, mandarins spread across Asia. India grows many varieties, especially Nagpur mandarins. Smaller and looser-skinned than oranges.
cautions: Acidic; avoid enamel erosion by rinsing mouth.
Mandarin.india_note: Nagpur famous; grown across central India.
tips: Heavy for size = juicy; bright color.
fun_facts: Contains synephrine for mild metabolism boost.

Cranberry.tagline: Tart UTI warrior with serious health cred.
Cranberry.about: Native to North American bogs, cranberries were used medicinally by indigenous peoples. Limited cultivation in India; mostly imported dried or juiced. Famous for juice and sauce.
benefits: UTI prevention — PACs stop E. coli sticking to bladder.
cautions: High acidity erodes enamel; drink with straw.
tips: Freeze whole or dry for snacks.
fun_facts: Native Americans mixed with meat for pemmican.

Blackberry.tagline: Wild, thorny treasure packed with dark antioxidants.
Blackberry.about: Blackberries grow wild across temperate zones; cultivated since Romans. In India, found in Himalayas and cultivated in Nilgiris. Larger, firmer than raspberries.
cautions: Seeds rough on digestion if overeaten.
Wild ones may need pesticide wash.
Blackberry.india_note: Wild in hills; cultivated in south.

Custard Apple.tagline: Creamy, sweet custard without the bake.
Custard Apple.about: Native to Americas but thrived in Indian tropics. Widely grown in Maharashtra, Bihar, and Andhra Pradesh. Flesh scoops out easily around large seeds.
Custard Apple.india_note: Major fruit in central and eastern India.
fun_facts: Seeds used as fish poison traditionally.

Sapota.tagline: Chewy, brown sugar sweetness in tropical skin.
Sapota.about: Also called chikoo or sapodilla. Brought to India by Portuguese; now major crop in Gujarat and Maharashtra. Latex used for chicle (original chewing gum).
fun_facts: Chicle from sap made first Chiclets.

Tamarind.about: African native naturalized across Indian tropics 4,000 years ago. Every part used: pods for souring, leaves medicinally. India produces 50% world supply.

Physalis.tagline: Golden berries in lantern husks — tart-sweet surprise.
Physalis.about: Also cape gooseberry or groundcherry. Grows easily in Indian hills; popular in Uttarakhand. Husk ripens fruit and protects from pests.
tips: Dry husk, orange fruit inside = ripe.

Rambutan.tagline: Hairy lychee cousin with translucent sweet flesh.
Rambutan.about: Name means "hairy" in Malay. Thrives in Kerala, Tamil Nadu backyards. Related to lychee and longan; husk peels back easily when ripe.
cautions: Seed mildly toxic raw; spit out.

Persimmon.tagline: Tomato-like orbs — sweet when ripe, astringent when not.
Persimmon.about: Ancient Chinese fruit; Fuyu (non-astringent) vs Hachiya (astringent). Limited in India; grown in Himachal hills. Called "date plum" by Romans.

Pomelo.about: Largest citrus; ancestor of grapefruit. Grown in Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu. Thick rind, airy segments with tough membranes.

Jujube.tagline: Date-like drupe — sweet, apple-crisp or chewy-dried.
Jujube.about: 4,000-year Chinese history; called ber in India. Grows across dry India. Used in Ayurveda for sleep, digestion.

Star Gooseberry.tagline: Tiny tart stars for spicy Indian pickles.
Star Gooseberry.about: Grows wild across Indian countryside. Called amla-nellikai in Tamil Nadu. Five ridges give star shape when cut.

Longan.about: Ancient Chinese medicine fruit (gui yuan = "dragon eye"). Limited cultivation in south India. Peel reveals white flesh around brown seed.

Loquat.about: South China native; Japan popularized. Grows in Nilgiris, Coorg. Flowers in fall, fruits winter — opposite seasons.

Kumquat.tagline: Eat whole mini-oranges — skin sweet, flesh tart.
Kumquat.about: Ancient Chinese; name = "golden orange." Grown in south India hills. Eaten whole or candied.

Salak.tagline: Snake fruit — scaly skin, crunchy sweet flesh.
Salak.about: Java palm clusters; trunkless. Rare in India; experimental Kerala. Peel like banana, three lobes.

Ackee.tagline: Jamaica's national fruit — creamy pods like scrambled eggs.
Ackee.about: Named after Captain Bligh. Jamaica staple; rare elsewhere. Unripe pods toxic (hypoglycin).
cautions: Cook only fully opened red pods.

Breadfruit.tagline: Starchy tree potato — Captain Bligh's bounty mutiny star.
Breadfruit.about: Polynesian staple; Bligh transported to Caribbean. Rare in India; Kerala trials. Latex tree; fruit like giant bread-like orb.

Carambola.tagline: Starfruit alias — crisp citrus stars (see Starfruit).
Carambola.about: Synonym for starfruit; same fruit, different name.
Carambola.india_note: Widely grown as Starfruit; same fruit.

Cherimoya.tagline: Ice cream in green scales — custard king.
Cherimoya.about: Inca delicacy; Mark Twain's favorite. Rare in India; Coorg/Kodaikanal. Scaly green skin, spoonable flesh.

Sapodilla.tagline: Brown on the outside, caramel-sweet on the inside — nature’s dessert fruit.
Sapodilla.about: Sapodilla, also known as chikoo, is a tropical fruit with grainy, brown flesh and a rich caramel-like sweetness. It was originally cultivated in Central America and later spread to India, where it is widely grown. The fruit is highly valued for its natural sugars and pleasant taste.
micronutrients: Vitamin C — boosts immunity and skin health.
Dietary Fiber — supports digestion and gut health.
benefits: Energy boost — natural sugars provide quick energy.
cautions: Unripe fruit contains latex and can irritate the mouth.
High sugar content — consume in moderation.
Sapodilla.india_note: Widely grown in Maharashtra, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, and Karnataka.
Sapodilla.global_note: Grown year-round in tropical climates worldwide.
tips: Choose slightly soft fruits with no cracks.
Store at room temperature until ripe.
culinary_tips: Use in desserts like ice cream or halwa.
fun_facts: Used to make natural chewing gum (chicle).
Can live and produce fruit for over 100 years.

Santol.tagline: A tangy-sweet tropical fruit with cottony pulp and a refreshing bite.
Santol.about: Santol is a tropical fruit popular in Southeast Asia, known for its thick rind and soft, cotton-like pulp. It has a balance of sweet and sour flavors and is often eaten fresh or used in cooking. The fruit is also valued for its medicinal uses in traditional remedies.
cautions: Seeds should not be swallowed — may cause digestive issues.
Santol.india_note: Rare in India; mostly found in Northeast regions.
Santol.global_note: Common in Thailand, Philippines, and Indonesia.
tips: Choose fruits with firm skin and no cracks.
Eat pulp fresh or add to dishes.
culinary_tips: Used in sour curries and chutneys.
fun_facts: Also called cotton fruit due to pulp texture.
Popular street snack in Southeast Asia.

Mangaba.tagline: A rare Brazilian fruit with a creamy texture and exotic aroma.
Mangaba.about: Mangaba is a tropical fruit native to Brazil, known for its soft pulp and fragrant aroma. It is widely used in juices, ice creams, and sweets. The fruit is delicate and spoils quickly, making it rare outside its native regions.
cautions: Unripe fruit may be toxic — consume only when ripe.
Mangaba.india_note: Not grown in India; rarely available.
tips: Eat fully ripe fruit for best flavor.
Handle gently due to soft skin.
culinary_tips: Blend into juices and ice creams.

Canistel.tagline: Egg fruit with a rich, custard-like texture and sweet flavor.
Canistel.about: Canistel, also known as egg fruit, has a dense, creamy texture similar to cooked egg yolk. It is sweet and mildly flavored, often used in desserts and smoothies. The fruit is popular in tropical regions and valued for its unique texture.
micronutrients: Vitamin A — supports vision and skin health.
cautions: Dry texture may feel heavy if eaten in large amounts.
Canistel.india_note: Rare but grown in some tropical regions of India.
Canistel.global_note: Cultivated in tropical America and Southeast Asia.
tips: Eat when soft and fully ripe.
fun_facts: Called egg fruit due to texture.
Often used as a natural dessert base.
